_comment: FRAGMENTS are the physical unit: a disjoint partition of one corpus build.
Every source belongs to exactly one fragment, so no byte of text exists in
two downloadable files. This is what keeps row ids safe to merge without
renumbering, and it is what stops a reader who installs 'Church Fathers'
and then 'Augustine of Hippo' from downloading Augustine twice.
COLLECTIONS are what the reader chooses: named, overlapping groupings that
reference fragments. A source can appear in as many collections as make
sense — Augustine is in 'Augustine of Hippo', 'Church Fathers' and
'Patristic era' — because a collection owns no text, only a list of
fragments. Installing one fetches the fragments not already present;
removing one drops only the fragments no other installed collection needs.
Adding a way to browse the corpus therefore costs a few lines here and no
Fragment selection is by tradition slug and/or exact author name; first
match wins, in declaration order. Anything unclaimed falls into 'core'.

collections.name: Creeds & Confessions
collections.description: What each tradition officially teaches: the ecumenical creeds, the canons of Trent, the Book of Concord, the Westminster standards, Heidelberg, Dordt, the Belgic Confession the Thirty-Nine Articles and the Second London Baptist Confession. The smallest set that can answer a question comparing two traditions.

collections.name: Church Fathers
collections.description: The complete ante-Nicene and post-Nicene fathers — every patristic author in the library, from Clement of Rome to John of Damascus.

collections.name: Medieval Theology
collections.description: The scholastic era: Aquinas's Summa Theologiae, the systematic work that shaped Catholic theology and the arguments the Reformers answered.

collections.name: Ante-Nicene Writers
collections.description: The fathers who wrote before the Council of Nicaea in 325: Tertullian, Origen, Clement of Alexandria and their contemporaries.

collections.name: Nicene & Post-Nicene Writers
collections.description: The fathers of the fourth and fifth centuries, when the creeds took their final form: Athanasius, the Cappadocians, Ambrose, Chrysostom and Augustine.

collections.name: Thomas Aquinas
collections.description: The complete Summa Theologiae in English — all five parts, 611 questions and 3,115 articles, each with its objections, replies and the argument between them.

collections.name: Augustine of Hippo
collections.description: The Confessions, City of God, the anti-Pelagian and anti-Donatist works, the homilies and the letters.

collections.name: John Chrysostom
collections.description: The homilies and treatises, including the complete series on Matthew, John, Romans and the Pauline epistles.

collections.name: Tertullian
collections.description: The apologetic, doctrinal and moral works of the first major Latin theologian.

collections.name: Origen
collections.description: Against Celsus, De Principiis and the commentaries on John and Matthew.

collections.name: Athanasius
collections.description: On the Incarnation, the discourses against the Arians, and the festal letters.

collections.name: Gregory of Nyssa
collections.description: Against Eunomius, On the Making of Man, and On the Soul and the Resurrection.

collections.name: Catholic
collections.description: The Council of Trent and the Catholic sources in the library, together with the Latin fathers the tradition rests on.

collections.name: Lutheran
collections.description: The Book of Concord — the Augsburg Confession and its Apology, the Smalcald Articles and Luther's catechisms.

collections.name: Reformed & Presbyterian
collections.description: The Westminster Confession and catechisms, the Heidelberg Catechism, the Canons of Dordt and the Belgic Confession.

collections.name: Baptist
collections.description: The Second London Baptist Confession of Faith (1689), the central Baptist confessional document.

collections.name: Eastern Orthodox
collections.description: Orthodox sources, together with the Greek fathers the tradition is grounded in.

collections.name: Methodist & Wesleyan
collections.description: John Wesley's Sermons on Several Occasions (1771) — with his notes on the New Testament, the doctrinal standard of Methodism — and the other Methodist sources in the library.
